steps.annotation: The computer starts executing code from here when you run the program
The machine follows this instruction, displaying the number 42
A program is a sequence of text instructions executed one after another
The computer reads each instruction from top to bottom, in order
C prints last because instructions always run sequentially
Source code lives in a plain text file with a .cpp extension
All your C++ instructions go inside the .cpp file
A compiler transforms your text source file into a runnable executable
The compiler translates every line into machine instructions the CPU understands
Double clicking the .exe launches the program starting at main
The processor runs each instruction in sequence until it reaches return
This text appears in the terminal, the window where program output is shown
All printed text shows up in the terminal so the user can read it
A prompt appears in the terminal asking the user to type something
The program waits for the user to type a response in the terminal
This directive brings in the I/O tools so you can use cout and cin
cout is only available because we included iostream on line 1
This shortcut means you can type cout instead of the full std::cout
Without the using line, you would need to write std::cout here
main is required; the operating system looks for it to start your program
Everything inside main executes when the program launches
A semicolon terminates each statement; omitting it causes a compiler error
This statement also requires a semicolon at the end
The { brace opens a block that groups multiple statements together
All code between the opening { and closing } belongs to this block
This line is inside the block that was opened on line 3
The } brace closes the block; every opening { needs a matching }
return 0 signals success to the operating system and exits the program
After return 0, no more lines inside main execute
Text after // is a comment that the compiler skips entirely
Only this executable code runs; comments are notes for the programmer
Spaces between words and symbols do not change the meaning of the code
Zero spaces also works; the compiler ignores extra whitespace
Indentation helps humans read code but the compiler ignores it
This deeper indent compiles identically; indentation is style, not syntax
cout is the standard output stream that displays text on screen
The word goodbye appears in the terminal when this line runs
price stores the value 9.99
The << operator pushes the value of price (9.99) to the output stream
Words inside double quotes form a string literal printed exactly as typed
The terminal shows: welcome to C++ (no quotes in the output)
Numeric values are printed directly; 100 appears on screen as digits
Output runs together without a separator, producing "1007" on screen
endl inserts a line break, moving the cursor down to the next line
Each endl ensures the next output starts on a fresh line
The \n escape character inside the string creates a new line on output
\n does the same job as endl but is written inside the quoted string
Multiple << operators chain values together on one output line
Produces "Ada is 21" by sending each piece to cout in sequence
score holds 95, which we want to display using printf
printf uses a format string with placeholders; %d is replaced by score
The %d placeholder tells printf to insert an integer at that spot
Output: "total = 42" because %d was replaced by the value of total
city is a C++ string that we want to print with printf
%s formats a C string; call .c_str() to convert the C++ string first
\n inside the printf format string starts a new line after printing
"second" prints on a separate line because the previous \n moved down
The first argument to printf is always the format string template
"value = %d\n" is the template; %d marks where 77 gets inserted
Each %d placeholder is matched to the next argument: 4, 5, then 20
Produces "4 x 5 = 20" by filling placeholders left to right
A variable is a labeled storage box; score is a box currently holding 100
The box contents can be replaced; score now holds 80
Names like "width" describe the purpose of the variable
Separate names identify separate boxes; pick names that explain the data
The type determines what kind of value the variable can store
int for whole numbers, double for decimals, string for text
A declaration reserves a box; the pattern is: type name;
The box exists but has no meaningful value yet until you assign one
Initialization creates the box and fills it in one step: type name = value;
Both the box and the starting value are set at the same time
total holds the number 42 in memory
Reading total retrieves 42 from the box without altering it
count starts with 3
Writing replaces the old value; count is now 7 (3 is gone forever)
A single = assigns a value to the variable (store operation)
Double == compares two values and returns true or false
You must declare a variable before referencing it in later lines
a can be used here because it was declared on the previous line
int stores whole numbers only: no decimals, no text
Any whole number (positive, negative, or zero) fits in an int
double is for numbers with fractional parts, like 72.5
Whenever a value has a decimal point, use double instead of int
string stores a sequence of characters, meaning any text value
Put text in double quotes to create a string value to store
A bool variable only holds true or false, nothing else
Bool values are used for flags and conditions; cout prints 1 or 0
char stores a single character, surrounded by single quotes
Letters, digits, and symbols each count as one character for char
2026 is an integer literal: plain digits typed directly in the code
Int literals are just numbers with no decimal point and no quotes
0.1 is a double literal because it contains a decimal point
The decimal point distinguishes a double literal from an int literal
Text inside double quotes is a string literal: a fixed piece of text
A string literal can go directly into cout without storing it first
A char literal uses single quotes around exactly one character
Single quotes for one character (char), double quotes for text (string)
true is a built in keyword, not a string; no quotes are needed
The only two bool literals are true and false; no other values exist
You must include the string header to use the string type
Without #include <string>, declaring a string variable fails to compile
cin is the input stream; it reads what the user types in the terminal
After typing and pressing Enter, the value is stored in temp
An empty box waiting to receive a value from the user
>> takes the next typed value from input and places it into price
Display a message so the user knows what to type
The program pauses here, waiting for the user to type and press Enter
Two boxes declared for two input values
The user types "3.5 7.2"; whitespace separates the two values
cin converts typed text to the variable type; digits become a double
For a string, cin grabs one word (stopping at the next space)
Chaining >> reads multiple values from one input line in sequence
All three values are now stored and ready to use in expressions
getline reads everything until Enter, including spaces in the middle
Unlike cin >>, getline captures the full line: "123 Main St" stays intact
The + operator computes the sum: 10 plus 25 equals 35
Outputs 35, the result of adding the two numbers
The minus sign subtracts the right side from the left: 100 minus 37 is 63
Displays 63, the difference between the two values
The * operator multiplies two values: 12 times 4 equals 48
Prints 48, the product of price and qty
The / operator divides: 20 divided by 4 gives 5
Prints 5, the quotient of 20 and 4
The % operator finds the remainder: 10 divided by 4 leaves 2
Outputs 2, the amount left over after dividing evenly
Dividing two ints drops the decimal: 7/2 gives 3, not 3.5
Prints 3 because integer division always rounds toward zero
Multiplication happens before addition: 3*2=6, then 4+6=10
Prints 10 because * has higher precedence than +
Parentheses make 4+3 evaluate first (=7), then 7*2=14
Outputs 14 because parentheses changed the evaluation order
+= means score = score + 3; a shorthand that adds and assigns at once
*= means score = score * 2; score was 13, now becomes 26
++ increments by 1; this is the same as count = count + 1
Prints 6 because ++ added 1 to the original value of 5
Decrements by 1; equivalent to lives = lives - 1
Prints 2 because the decrement reduced lives from 3 to 2
== checks whether two values are identical; here 10 equals 10, so true
The body runs because the equality test returned true
!= returns true when the two sides are different values (21 is not 18)
This runs because the not equal check evaluated to true
< returns true when the left side is smaller than the right (15 < 20)
Prints because 15 is less than 20
> checks whether the left value exceeds the right (85 > 70 is true)
The body executes because 85 is greater than 70
<= is true when the left side is less than or equal to the right
18 <= 18 is true because the equal case is included
>= is true when the left side is greater than or the same as the right
100 >= 100 is true because the values match (equal case counts)
Any comparison produces a bool (true or false) that you can save
The stored bool can be used directly as a condition later
&& requires both conditions to be true; if either is false, the whole thing is false
Both age >= 18 and hasID are true, so the AND passes
|| needs just one side to be true; the whole condition passes if either holds
rain is true, so the OR succeeds even though snow is false
! inverts the bool value: !true becomes false, !false becomes true
This does not print because !done is false (done was true)
The if statement evaluates the condition and runs the body only if true
30 > 25 is true, so this line executes
40 >= 50 is false, so the if body is skipped
else provides an alternative path when the if condition fails
This line runs because score was below 50 and the else branch was taken
First check: 75 >= 80 is false, so skip to the next condition
Second check: 75 >= 70 is true, so this branch runs and the rest are skipped
The comparison result (true or false) is stored in a bool variable
if requires a bool expression; the saved variable works directly
Braces are recommended around every if body for clarity and safety
Using {} even for one line avoids accidental errors when editing later
while repeats the body as long as the condition stays true
Prints 3 2 1 as count decreases each time through the loop
When count hits 0, the condition becomes false and the loop stops
do/while always runs the body once before testing the condition
After the body runs, the condition is checked; if true, repeat
The for loop packs initialization, condition, and step into one line
Prints 1 2 3; the for loop is the most commonly used loop structure
The init part (int i = 10) executes just once at the very start
Prints 10 11 12; the init value determines where counting begins
The condition (i < 3) is evaluated before each pass through the body
When i reaches 3, the condition fails and the loop exits
The step (i++) executes after the body finishes, before rechecking the condition
Order each cycle: check condition, run body, then run step
break stops the loop immediately and jumps past the closing brace
Only prints 0 1 2 3 4 because break exits before i reaches 5
continue skips the remaining body and proceeds to the next iteration
Output: 0 1 2 4 5; the number 3 is skipped because continue jumped ahead
The outer loop runs twice, once for each row
The inner loop runs 3 times per outer pass; total runs: 2 times 3 = 6
true as the condition means the loop will repeat forever unless stopped
An infinite loop must have a break or changing condition to exit safely
A function packages reusable code under a name you can call later
Calling say_bye() transfers control to the function, runs it, then comes back
The signature pattern: returnType name(params) { body } defines a function
This function takes two doubles, multiplies them, and returns the result
Arguments 2.5 and 4.0 are passed in; the returned 10.0 is stored in result
Prints 10, the value the function computed and returned
val is its own local box containing a copy of the argument
Modifying val only affects the copy; price in main remains 9.99
The return type (double) declares what kind of value the function sends back
return delivers the computed result (27.0) back to the calling code
void means the function performs an action but does not return a value
It prints output but gives nothing back to the caller
A prototype tells the compiler about the function before it is fully defined
The complete definition appears later; the prototype bridged the gap
A prototype needs only return type, name, and parameter types; names are optional
Declares that show takes an int and returns nothing (void)
temp is created when compute starts and exists only while it runs
When the function ends, temp is destroyed and its memory is freed
result is local to compute; only code inside compute can access it
main cannot see result because it belongs to a different function scope
Without &, val receives a separate copy of x from the caller
Only the local copy changes; the original x in main remains 4
By value: val is a copy, completely independent from score
score is still 100 because the function only zeroed its own copy
The & makes val and x share the same memory location (an alias)
Since val IS x, multiplying val by 2 also changes x to 8
void f(int &val) means val operates on the caller variable directly
After the call, x is 8 because val and x shared the same box
With & on the parameter, changes here directly modify the caller variable
val is 15; the reference parameter made the mutation stick
OK: x is a real variable with a box that the reference can point to

steps.code: // double_it(10);  // ERROR: literal cannot bind to &
steps.annotation: 10 is just a value with no box, so a non const reference cannot bind to it
const & passes by reference for speed but blocks any modification
Reading is allowed; writing s.name = "X" would trigger a compiler error
val and x are two labels for the same storage; a reference is a second name
x shows 8; one box with two names (val inside the function, x in main)
An array stores multiple values of the same type in consecutive slots
Use an index to retrieve each value from the sequence
type arr[N] reserves space for N elements; here 3 double slots are created
Elements start uninitialized; assign a value before reading
Brace initialization sets all four elements at declaration time
Prints 3.3; element at index 2 is the third value in the list
Index 0 always refers to the very first element of the array
Prints 5 because arrays count from zero, not from one
The index can be a variable, not just a literal number
data[pos] looks up element 3, which is 8 (the fourth value)
Four elements at indices 0 through 3; zero based indexing
nums[0] is 5 (first) and nums[3] is 35 (last); indexing starts at 0
The last valid index is always N minus 1; here arr[4] gives 10
With 5 elements, valid indices are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
Accessing index N or beyond is undefined behavior; it may crash or give garbage
arr[3] is the highest safe index for a 4 element array
The size of a C array must be a compile time constant, not a variable
50 slots are created and the size can never grow or shrink at runtime
Since arrays cannot be returned, pass one as a parameter for the function to fill
Writing to arr modifies the caller array because arrays pass by reference
Use a for loop from 0 to N minus 1 to walk through every element
On each pass, i advances and nums[i] gives the next element
Assign a value to a specific slot using arr[index] = value;
Prints 50; the old value at index 1 has been overwritten
Pass an array with type arr[] and always include the size as a second parameter
The array name and its length are sent to the function
Arrays always pass by reference; the function accesses the original data
This write directly modifies the caller array; vals[0] becomes 0
A struct bundles related data fields into a single named package
Product groups label and price together as one unit
struct Color { ... }; creates a new custom data type named Color
Color has three integer fields: r, g, and b
The semicolon after the closing brace completes the type definition
Each field follows: type fieldName; like a normal variable declaration
A struct can have as many fields as needed, each with its own type
Curly braces enclose all the fields of the struct
Forgetting the ; after } is a common mistake that causes a compiler error
Declaring a variable with a struct type creates an instance with all fields
item now has its own label and price fields ready to use
The dot (.) accesses a specific field inside a struct instance
Reading r.w retrieves the stored value through the dot operator
structVar.field = value; stores a value in a named field of the struct
Each field is set individually using the dot assignment pattern
Write the value 5 into the h field of r
cout << structVar.field outputs the stored value (5) to the terminal
A struct field can be an array; marks holds 4 integers
A struct can mix array fields and scalar fields in the same definition
First use dot to reach the array, then brackets to select the element
structVar.arrayField[i] chains field access and array indexing together
Type name[N] creates an array of N struct instances
Each element in the array is a separate struct with its own fields
list[i].field combines an array index with a struct field in one expression
Each array slot has its own independent struct fields
By value: r is a full copy of box; they are independent structs
Only the copy is zeroed; box.w stays 10 in the caller
& means r is an alias for box; they share the same struct data
Doubling r.w also doubles box.w because & links them together
The & on the parameter allows the function to alter the caller struct
Setting s.total to 0 persists in data because & shares the memory
A struct array parameter lets the function fill the caller array directly
Writes through list[i] modify the original array since arrays pass by reference
Writing to list[i].field inside the function changes the caller array
Prints "Widget" because arrays are always passed by reference
const & avoids copying the struct while also preventing any changes to it
You can read fields freely; any write attempt would fail to compile
Hand execution means stepping through each line and recording variable states
Update your notes: a was 10, subtract 3, a is now 7
Note down: a = 3
Maintain a table of all variables: a = 3, b = 7, c = 10
Each assignment replaces the previous value; old one is lost
Trace: val went 1 -> 2 -> 3; the final state is 3
Record i on each pass: i=10 (runs), i=11 (runs), i=12 (runs), i=13 (stops)
Output trace: 10 11 12; loop exits when the condition becomes false
Test the condition: 15 > 25 is false, so skip to the else branch
The else runs because the condition evaluated to false
When tracing a call, start a new frame with the parameter set to the argument
Inside the frame: val = 10, so val * 2 = 20 is printed
tmp is alive only while work() is executing; track it in this frame
When work returns, delete tmp from your trace table; it no longer exists
Because of &, x and val share one box; adding 10 updates val too
Trace: val was 5, now 15; the & let the function change the caller variable
Trace the array: arr = {10, 20, 30}
slot is & for arr[1]; slot = 50 changes arr to {10, 50, 30}
b gets a copy of a (10); they are separate boxes now
Changing a to 20 does not touch b; final state: a = 20, b = 10
Trace: arr changes from {5, 5, 5} to {10, 5, 5}
Trace each pass: i=1 product=1, i=2 product=2, i=3 product=6
Final result: product = 6 (which is 1 * 1 * 2 * 3)
3 > 5 is false, so skip the if body and enter the else block
Trace: result = 0 (the else branch was taken because condition was false)
Enter double_it: n=7, compute 7*2=14, return 14 to the caller
Back in main: val = 14 (the returned result); output is 14
Value pass: copy is zeroed but a stays 10 in main
Reference pass: a itself becomes 0 because & shares the same box
Start min at first element (5); scan the rest for anything smaller
Trace: i=1 2<5 yes min=2, i=2 8<2 no, i=3 1<2 yes min=1; final min=1
Trace: box.w was 10, now 15; box.h was 20, now 25 (both via &)
Prints 15 25; the struct fields were mutated through the reference
Trace the accumulator each pass: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10, 10+5=15
mystery accumulates the total; after the loop it holds 15 (the sum)
Begin with the keyword struct, then your chosen type name, then {
Add each field that describes one piece of data about the entity
Finish the definition with }; so the compiler registers the new type
A field declaration is just like a variable: type followed by name then ;
Match the type to the kind of data: int for IDs, double for prices, string for text
Opening brace starts the list of fields
The }; is essential; leaving off the semicolon causes a compile error
Use int for whole number data like years and counts
Use string when the spec describes text data like names or titles
Use double for any value that might have a decimal component
Putting rating first is perfectly valid; field order does not affect behavior
The compiler accepts any arrangement of fields within the struct
A read function uses void because it fills data rather than returning a value
It accomplishes its purpose by writing into the passed array
Type list[] in the parameter passes the caller array for the function to fill
Each loop iteration populates one struct element in the array
The count parameter specifies how many items to read from input
The loop runs exactly count times; count limits how much of the array is filled
The loop iterates count times, processing one struct per pass
Each iteration fills one element; i advances to the next slot automatically
cin >> list[i].field stores user input directly into the struct element
A separate cin reads each additional field of the same struct
Each field needs its own cin statement; one per piece of input data
With three fields, three cin calls are required per loop iteration
A prompt before each cin is optional but helps the user know what to type
After the prompt, cin reads the value the user provides
int main() is the required entry point for every C++ program
End main with return 0 to tell the OS everything finished normally
const makes MAX a fixed constant; its value can never be reassigned
Use the constant to size the array so the capacity is easy to change later
Create an array of your struct type sized by the constant MAX
Each slot is a complete struct ready to store data
Declare count to hold the number of records the user will enter
Read the actual count from the user so the program knows how many to process
The user types how many items there are; this number drives the loops
count is now set and will be passed to functions that need it
Main calls the read function, passing the array and user provided count
The function handles input; main coordinates the overall flow
Loop from 0 to count to output each stored item
Print each record inside the loop using printf or cout
%d prints the int field, %s prints the string field from the struct
Each format specifier is matched to a corresponding struct field value
printf cannot directly accept a C++ string; conversion is needed
Call .c_str() to get the C style string pointer that %s requires
The struct holds mystery plus two input values x and y
mystery stores the product of x and y; a straightforward multiplication pattern
Start with the first element, then scan for the smallest value
Update mystery whenever a smaller element is found; this finds the minimum
Reset the accumulator before summing all elements
After the loop, dividing by 5 gives the average; this is a mean calculator
mystery holds the arithmetic mean of all five values
Assume x is the larger value to start with
Check whether y beats x; if so, mystery becomes y instead
mystery ends up holding the larger of x and y; a max of two pattern
Start the counter at zero before scanning the array
Test each element for a condition (negative in this case)
Increment by 1 for each match; mystery ends as the count of negatives
